pdf.text: Madison Clinical Persona Pipeline Assignment 3 — Data Collection Documentation Raksha Krishna Moorthy INFO7375 Branding and AI | Northeastern University Live Report: https://rakshakmoorthy.github.io/madison-persona-pipeline/ Executive Summary This document describes the data collection pipeline built for Assignment 3 of INFO7375 Branding and AI. The pipeline collects, validates, and organizes clinical patient data to power the Madison SyntheticPersonas project — an AI tool that generates clinically-grounded patient personas for health-fashion brands like Tommy Hilfiger Adaptive, Aerie, and MastMe. The pipeline pulls from four Tier 1 public data sources and merges them into a single clean dataset of 125 records across 5 health-fashion categories. All data is validated, deduplicated, and standardized before output. Metric Value Total records 125 Quality rate 100% Quality status ✓ PASS (threshold: 80%+) Data sources 4 (Kaggle, CDC, HuggingFace, RSS) Health-fashion categories 5 Fields per record 10 Duplicates removed 0 Date format YYYY-MM-DD (consistent) Pipeline Architecture The workflow is built in n8n and consists of 12 nodes in 4 layers. All 4 data sources run in parallel, converge into a merge node, then pass through quality checking and output generation. Each source fills a distinct dimension. Kaggle provides patient demographics. CDC provides population-level prevalence. HuggingFace provides clinical treatment stage context. RSS provides real-world patient language. Together they give Assignment 4 everything needed to generate complete personas. Part 1: Data Inventory Project: Madison SyntheticPersonas — Clinical Persona Pipeline Problem: Health-fashion brands like Tommy Hilfiger Adaptive, Aerie, and MastMe lack clinically-grounded patient personas. Generic marketing tools miss the nuance of health identity — body image, treatment stage, emotional state, and fashion needs — for patients managing cancer, diabetes, alopecia, or mobility challenges. Source 1: Kaggle — Cancer Patients Data Type: Patient demographics CSV — age, gender, cancer severity (Low/Medium/High), symptom scores Amount: 100 records processed → 48 records after category balancing Purpose: Core demographic backbone — who the patient is, how severe their condition is, and clinical symptom profile Collection Method: File downloaded manually from kaggle.com/datasets/rishidamarla/cancer-patients-data, stored locally at ~/.n8n-files/kaggle_healthcare.csv, read by n8n Read/Write Files from Disk node Quality: 100/100 complete records — all fields present including Age, Gender, Level, Fatigue, Obesity, Smoking, Weight Loss Source 2: CDC — Chronic Disease Indicators Type: Public health JSON API — population-level chronic disease prevalence by state, year, gender, and ethnicity Amount: 100 API records → 40 records after category filtering Purpose: Population-level clinical context — validates real-world prevalence of each health-fashion category across demographics Collection Method: HTTP Request node to CDC Open Data API: data.cdc.gov/resource/hksd-2xuw.json — no authentication required Quality: 40/40 complete records — condition, location, year, prevalence value, and gender stratification present in every record Source 3: HuggingFace — Augmented Clinical Notes Type: Clinical NLP dataset — de-identified clinical case notes with treatment context Amount: 100 API records → 25 records after keyword filtering Purpose: Treatment stage depth — provides clinical language context (newly-diagnosed, active-treatment, post-treatment, monitoring) that demographics alone cannot provide Collection Method: HTTP Request to HuggingFace Datasets Server: datasets-server.huggingface.co. Dataset: AGBonnet/augmented-clinical-notes — no authentication required Quality: 25/25 complete records — condition, treatment_stage, and clinical notes present in every record Source 4: RSS Feed — ScienceDaily Breast Cancer News Type: Health news RSS feed — articles about breast cancer research, treatment, and survivorship Amount: 30+ feed items → 12 records after keyword matching Purpose: Real-world patient community signals — how patients and researchers talk about conditions, feeding the communication tone dimension of persona generation Collection Method: HTTP Request to ScienceDaily RSS: sciencedaily.com/rss/health_medicine/breast_cancer.xml — no authentication required Quality: 12/12 complete records — article title, date (YYYY-MM-DD), source, category, and fashion signal present in every record Data Schema Field Type Example Purpose record_id String kag_7rcm7zh9s Unique identifier with source prefix source String kaggle Data origin (kaggle/cdc/huggingface/rss) category String post-mastectomy One of 5 health-fashion categories age Integer/null 37 Patient age (Kaggle only; null for others) gender String/null Female Patient gender (Kaggle only; null for others) condition String cancer — severity: High Human-readable condition name treatment_stage String active-treatment Stage in treatment journey fashion_signal String post-surgical bras, front-closure tops Relevant fashion needs for category date_collected String 2026-05-28 Collection date in YYYY-MM-DD format notes String Smoking:7 Obesity:7 Fatigue:4 Additional clinical context Statistics Metric Value Total records collected 125 Clean records 125 Quality rate 100% Quality threshold 80%+ — PASS Duplicates removed 0 Date format YYYY-MM-DD (consistent) Data sources 4 (all Tier 1 public) Fields per record 10 Category Records Sources Post-Mastectomy 30 Kaggle + HuggingFace + RSS Alopecia 30 Kaggle only Diabetes 12 Kaggle + CDC + HuggingFace Adaptive Mobility 23 CDC + HuggingFace Cancer Recovery 30 Kaggle + CDC + HuggingFace Note: Alopecia records are derived from Kaggle low-severity cancer patients. RSS and HuggingFace clinical notes for this specific category were limited in the available public datasets. All 30 alopecia records are complete and valid. Ready for: Assignment 4 — Madison SyntheticPersonas Persona Generation Pipeline. This data will feed an LLM pipeline generating clinically-grounded synthetic patient personas for health-fashion brands. Live Interactive Report: https://rakshakmoorthy.github.io/madison-persona-pipeline/ Part 2: Setup Guide Everything needed to run this workflow on a Mac. No paid accounts or API keys required. What you need • Node.js v18+ and npm v9+ (nodejs.org) • n8n installed: npm install -g n8n • The Kaggle CSV file placed at: ~/.n8n-files/kaggle_healthcare.csv • The workflow JSON: KrishnaMoorthy_Raksha_A3_Workflow.json Step 1 — Install n8n npm install -g n8n Step 2 — Get the Kaggle data file 1. Go to kaggle.com/datasets/rishidamarla/cancer-patients-data 2. Click Download — you get archive.zip 3. Unzip and rename the CSV to: kaggle_healthcare.csv 4. Run in Terminal: mkdir -p ~/.n8n-files && cp ~/Downloads/kaggle_healthcare.csv ~/.n8n-files/ Step 3 — Start n8n n8n start Open browser to: http://localhost:5678 Create an account (any email and password). Step 4 — Import and run the workflow 5. Three-dots menu (top right) → Import from file → select the workflow JSON 6. All 12 nodes appear on canvas 7. Click Execute workflow — all nodes turn green 8. Click Code: Summary Report to see 125 records at 100% quality Data source credentials No credentials needed. All sources are public: • Kaggle — CSV read from local disk, no API calls • CDC — public open data API, no authentication • HuggingFace — public datasets server, no authentication • RSS — public XML feed, no authentication Common errors Error Fix Access to file not allowed Move kaggle_healthcare.csv to ~/.n8n-files/ CDC returns 0 records Turn off Execute Once in Code: Merge + Quality Check settings Merge shows only 1 source Turn Execute Once OFF in the Merge + Quality Check node RSS connection error URL: sciencedaily.com/rss/health_medicine/breast_cancer.xml jszip module disallowed All nodes use pure JavaScript — no imports needed Part 3: Data Quality Summary Quality Check Requirement Result Records complete 80%+ with no missing critical fields 125/125 — 100% PASS Essential fields present condition, date, source in every record All 10 fields in every record — PASS Duplicates removed No repeated entries 0 duplicates — PASS Date format Consistent YYYY-MM-DD 100% consistent — PASS Data relevance Relevant to health-fashion project All 5 categories covered — PASS Built-in validation (Code: Merge + Quality Check node) • Validates category against 5 allowed values — rejects invalid records • Checks all required fields exist before accepting a record • Deduplicates by record_id automatically • Auto-corrects date format to YYYY-MM-DD • Balances categories — maximum 30 records per category • Each parse node returns empty array if source is unavailable — workflow never crashes • Logs quality rate percentage and pass/fail status to console Final result: 125 clean records | 100% quality | 4 Tier 1 sources | 5 categories | 0 duplicates | 2026

Madison Clinical Persona Pipeline Data Quality Metrics Raksha Krishna Moorthy | INFO7375 Branding and AI | Overview The Madison Clinical Persona Pipeline dataset is designed to be persona-ready, not just aggregated. All 125 records are normalized into a consistent 10-field schema across four clinical data sources — Kaggle, CDC, HuggingFace, and RSS — enabling reliable LLM-driven persona generation in Assignment 4. The dataset prioritizes truthful representation of source data, explicitly modeling unknowns using null values instead of fabricating demographic fields that the source data does not contain. Data Structure & Consistency Unified Schema All 125 records — regardless of source — share the same 10-field structure: Core fields (present in every record): • record_id — unique identifier with source prefix • source — data origin (kaggle / cdc / huggingface / rss) • category — one of 5 valid health-fashion values • condition — human-readable condition name • treatment_stage — stage in treatment journey • fashion_signal — relevant fashion needs for the category • date_collected — ISO-8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD) • notes — additional clinical or contextual context Demographically-conditional fields (null by design for non-clinical sources): • age — numeric for Kaggle patient records; null for CDC, HuggingFace, RSS • gender — Male/Female for Kaggle; null for population-level and community sources This guarantees schema-level consistency across all 4 sources, which is critical for downstream LLM persona generation in Assignment 4. Data Cleaning Steps Applied • ✔ Duplicate Handling — duplicate record_ids removed; uniqueness enforced by source prefix + random string combination • ✔ Category Validation — every record validated against 5 allowed values; invalid categories rejected before merge • ✔ Field Normalization — condition names standardized to lowercase with consistent formatting; fashion signals mapped from category lookup table • ✔ Date Standardization — all records include date_collected in YYYY-MM-DD format; malformed dates auto-corrected in Merge + Quality Check node • ✔ Category Balancing — maximum 30 records per category enforced to prevent skew in persona generation • ✔ Fashion Signal Backfill — missing fashion_signal values auto-populated from category mapping rather than left blank • ✔ Source Unavailability Handling — each parse node returns empty array if source is down; workflow never crashes Data Relevance — Problem Alignment Each data source directly supports the core Madison problem: generating clinically-grounded patient personas for health-fashion brands. Source What it contributes Why it matters Kaggle Patient demographics — age, gender, cancer severity Who the patient is — persona backbone CDC Population-level condition prevalence by state and demographic Real-world scale and clinical context HuggingFace Clinical notes with treatment stage language Where the patient is in their journey RSS Health news and community articles How patients talk — persona voice and tone No extraneous fields or unrelated records are included. Every record maps directly to one of 5 health-fashion categories: Post-Mastectomy, Alopecia, Diabetes, Adaptive Mobility, or Cancer Recovery. Quality Metrics Dataset Summary Metric Value Total records collected 125 Clean records 125 Overall quality rate 100% Quality threshold 80%+ — PASS Sources 4 (all Tier 1 public) Duplicates removed 0 Date format compliance 100% YYYY-MM-DD Fields per record 10 Quality Checklist Quality Requirement Status 80%+ records complete ✓ 100% — PASS Required fields present in every record ✓ All 10 fields — PASS Duplicates removed ✓ 0 duplicates — PASS Dates standardized to ISO-8601 ✓ YYYY-MM-DD — PASS Data relevant to problem ✓ All 5 categories covered — PASS Category values validated ✓ Against 5 allowed values — PASS Fashion signals present ✓ All 125 records — PASS Records by Source and Category Source Records Category Coverage Kaggle 48 Post-Mastectomy, Alopecia, Diabetes, Cancer Recovery CDC 40 Diabetes, Adaptive Mobility, Cancer Recovery HuggingFace 25 Post-Mastectomy, Diabetes, Adaptive Mobility, Cancer Recovery RSS 12 Post-Mastectomy Total 125 All 5 categories covered Intentional Use of Null Values null values in this dataset are intentional and correct — they represent the absence of a metric that the source does not observe, not missing or corrupted data. Field Null in which sources Why age CDC, HuggingFace, RSS Population-level and news data does not contain individual patient age gender CDC, HuggingFace, RSS Population aggregates and clinical notes do not isolate individual gender This preserves data integrity and causal correctness. Fabricating age or gender for CDC population records would introduce false precision into the persona generation pipeline. All 8 other fields (record_id, source, category, condition, treatment_stage, fashion_signal, date_collected, notes) are present and non-null in every single record across all 125 rows. Conclusion The Madison Clinical Persona Pipeline dataset meets and exceeds all data quality requirements for Assignment 3: • Fully structured — consistent 10-field schema across all 4 sources • High completeness — 100% quality rate across all 125 records • Clinically grounded — real patient data, clinical notes, and population statistics • Explicit uncertainty handling — null values are documented design decisions, not errors • Ready for Assignment 4 — LLM persona generation pipeline can consume this data immediately This data will feed a persona generation pipeline that produces clinically-grounded synthetic patient personas for health-fashion brands like Tommy Hilfiger Adaptive, Aerie, and MastMe.
